what_it_does: This file is the module constitution for the contexts/ registry. It defines what a context is, how contexts self-register from per-file CONTEXT declarations, how they are addressed via context://<id>, how they resolve through the runtime address seam, lists the current live contexts, and prescribes rules for adding, removing, and documenting contexts.
observations: Contexts/ is treated as a file-discovered registry of reusable context blobs, analogous to skills and roles registries.
Each context is a module-level CONTEXT dict with required id and content and optional label/description; id must equal the file stem.
The address scheme is context://<id> and unknown ids must raise fail-loud.
The live set currently contains only company_overview.
Resolution is delegated to runtime/cognition.py:resolve_address, which dispatches context:// to runtime/skills.py:ContextRegistry.read(id).
Adding a context requires adding a file and updating this constitution; tests/skills_contexts_acceptance.py asserts the live set is reflected here.
Explicit prohibitions include no hardcoded literal lists, no second registry pattern, no fabrication for unknown ids, and no shipping without updating this drift home.
standouts: Registry-is-truth: contexts are registries like anything else.
context://<id> as an address seam with fail-loud resolution.
company_overview as the demonstrative first live context.
Mirror-don't-parallel: reuse the same registry pattern as skills and roles.
conventions: Follows the project constitution format with YAML front matter and status: living.
Uses a self-registering, one-file-per-entry registry pattern.
Uses URI-like address scheme dispatch through a centralized resolver.
Treats the constitution file as a drift-home that must stay synchronized with the file registry and acceptance tests.
concerns: The live-set list is currently a single item, so it is small and easy to drift from if not updated.
Heavy reliance on runtime/skills.py, runtime/cognition.py, and contracts/address.py without showing their interfaces in this file.
The opening definition 'a context is a declared, reusable unit of context' is circular and may confuse readers.
Terms like 'drift home' and 'Concurrent Cognition C 3b' are used without being defined locally.
notes: This is the constitution for the contexts/ registry: it mandates file-discovered, self-registering context declarations addressable via context:// and kept in sync with this document.
questions: What is the exact shared registry base class or machinery used by SkillRegistry, RoleRegistry, and ContextRegistry?
How does ContextRegistry discover and read .py files, and what triggers rediscovery?
What types or formats are allowed for the content field?
What specific assertions does tests/skills_contexts_acceptance.py make?
What does 'drift home' mean precisely in this project's terminology?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose matches content: it is a prescriptive constitution that documents and enforces the contexts registry design.
intention_signals: It aims to make reusable context blobs addressable inputs for roles by giving them their own discoverable registry and URI scheme, extending the existing address seam without changing the resolver, cognition driver, or UI.
novelty: It treats reusable context blobs as registry entries with a dedicated context:// scheme, distinct from skills (instructions) and roles, and makes the constitution file itself the synchronized drift home validated by acceptance tests.
contribution: It provides a clear prescriptive contract for the contexts module: the context schema, discovery mechanism, context:// address semantics, fail-loud resolution policy, recorded live context list, and guardrails against hardcoding and parallel registry patterns.
summary_for_embedding: company/contexts/AGENTS.md is the prescriptive constitution for a file-discovered, self-registering context registry where each contexts/<id>.py declares a CONTEXT dict and context://<id> resolves to its content via the runtime address seam.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to formalize how reusable context blobs are declared, discovered, addressed, and kept in sync so roles can consume them as inputs without hardcoding or UI changes.
